name: Fermium
name_origin: Named in honor of Enrico Fermi, the Italian-American physicist who built the first nuclear reactor.

discovery.discoverer: Albert Ghiorso and team at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory; identified in 'Ivy Mike' thermonuclear test debris

occurrence.abundance_summary: Synthetic element not found in nature; produced in picogram to nanogram quantities by intense neutron irradiation of lower actinide targets, or in bombardment experiments using accelerators.

compounds.name: Fermium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Inferred trichloride identified only in trace ion-exchange and coprecipitation studies; characterized chemically rather than structurally.

uses: Scientific research into heavy-element chemistry and nuclear physics, including studies of spontaneous fission
Probes for measuring f-electron behavior at the heavy end of the actinide series
reactivity_summary: Bulk metallic fermium has never been prepared due to the tiny quantities available. Its chemistry has been studied entirely on the tracer scale and is dominated by the +3 oxidation state, with a more accessible +2 state than seen in lighter actinides.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Fermium-257 has the longest half-life (100.5 days). The 'fermium gap' is a well-known limit: above mass 258, fermium isotopes undergo extremely rapid spontaneous fission, preventing further neutron-capture buildup of heavier elements via slow processes.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
